title: Potted crab
subtitle: Crab meat set in spiced butter, served with toast
excerpt: Potted crab is crab meat packed into ramekins, covered with clarified spiced butter, and chilled until firm. It is one of the simplest things you can make with fresh crab, and one of the most satisfying: cold, rich, and clean at the same time.
sourceNotes: Mrs Beeton, Book of Household Management (1861, Project Gutenberg #10136). Potted crab entry, method of mixing crab meat with butter and mace. Eliza Acton, Modern Cookery for Private Families (1845, Project Gutenberg). Devilled crab preparation, use of cayenne with shellfish.

recipe.makeAheadNotes: Can be made up to 2 days ahead. Keep chilled, covered with cling film. Remove from the fridge 10 minutes before serving so the butter softens slightly.

glossaryTerms.definition: Butter that has been melted and the milk solids and water removed, leaving pure butterfat. It sets firmer than whole butter and keeps longer without going rancid.

content.text: Potted crab is crab meat mixed with spiced butter, packed into small pots, and sealed with a layer of
. A few hours in the fridge and it is ready to eat: cold, rich, and clean. Serve it with plenty of toast and a wedge of lemon.

Good crab matters here. Use freshly picked white and brown meat if you can get it, or good-quality tinned crab as a second choice. The flavour of the potting butter is built from mace and cayenne, which warm without overpowering.

items.prepNote: mixed white and brown, picked over for shell

content.text: You need four small ramekins or one larger pot. Toast or thin crackers to serve.
Melt the butter gently in a small saucepan over a low heat without stirring. When fully melted, a white foam will rise to the top and the milk solids will sink to the bottom.
Skim the foam from the surface with a spoon. Carefully pour the clear yellow butter into a jug, leaving the white sediment in the bottom of the pan. You now have clarified butter.
Put about two-thirds of the clarified butter back into the pan over a very low heat. Add the mace, cayenne, and lemon juice. Stir for 30 seconds.
Add the crab meat and fold it through gently. Taste and add salt as needed. The mixture should be richly flavoured with a mild heat at the back.
Spoon the crab mixture into the ramekins, pressing it down lightly so there are no air pockets. Leave a 5 mm gap at the top for the sealing butter.
Pour the remaining clarified butter over the top to form a thin sealing layer. Refrigerate for at least 1 hour until the butter is firm.

items.cause: The ramekins were moved while the butter was still liquid, or the fridge was too cold.
items.fix: A cracked seal does not affect the taste. Eat within 2 days regardless, as potted crab has no long shelf life once open.

items.cause: Too little salt, or the crab quality was low.
items.fix: Season the mixture before potting. Brown crab meat adds more depth than white alone. Use a pinch more mace if the flavour still reads flat.

items.symptom: The mixture is too soft to spread
items.cause: The butter was too warm when mixed with the crab, giving a loose texture.
items.fix: Chill it. Once cold the mixture firms up to a spreadable paste. There is no harm in a slightly loose texture.

items.cause: Fresh crab was not picked carefully enough.
items.fix: Run your fingers through the meat gently before mixing. Any fragments feel hard against your fingertip. Take your time; one shard ruins the pleasure.

content.text: Potted crab belongs to the same British tradition as potted shrimp from Morecambe Bay, which uses brown shrimp rather than crab. The practice of sealing shellfish in spiced clarified butter goes back at least to the 18th century, when it served as a means of preservation without refrigeration. Today it works as a first course or a lunch: cold, extravagant in a quiet way, and very quick to prepare.
